title: Character and Logical Types
description: Explore character strings and logical (boolean) values.

slides.title: Character Type (Strings) 📝
slides.content:
# Character Type

Character data is used to store text. In R, you create character values by enclosing text in either double quotes `"` or single quotes `'`.

```r
name <- "Alice"
city <- 'Paris'
```

You can check if an object is character with `is.character()`.

## Special Characters

Use `\n` for newline, `\t` for tab. To include a quote inside a string, escape it with backslash or use the other quote type:

```r
# Using escape
text <- "She said, \"Hello\""
# Alternating quotes
text2 <- 'She said, "Hello"'
```

slides.title: Character Examples
slides.content: Run this code to see character handling:
slides.codeSnippet:
greeting <- "Hello, World!"
print(greeting)
print(is.character(greeting))

escaped <- "Line1\nLine2"
cat(escaped)  # cat() shows escape sequences

quizData.question: Which of the following is a valid character string in R?
quizData.explanation: Strings must be enclosed in matching quotes. 'World' is fine. "Hello is missing closing quote. Hello without quotes is treated as an object name. The last option has unescaped inner quotes.

slides.title: Logical Type (Booleans) ✅❌
slides.content:
Logical values represent TRUE or FALSE. They are often the result of comparisons.

```r
a <- 5 > 3   # TRUE
b <- 10 == 9 # FALSE
```

Logical values can also be written as `T` and `F`, but using `TRUE`/`FALSE` is safer because `T` and `F` can be overwritten.

## Logical Operators

* `&` (AND) – TRUE if both are TRUE
* `|` (OR)  – TRUE if at least one is TRUE
* `!` (NOT) – reverses logical value

```r
TRUE & FALSE  # FALSE
TRUE | FALSE  # TRUE
!TRUE         # FALSE
```

slides.title: Logical in Action
slides.codeSnippet:
x <- 10
y <- 5
print(x > y)   # TRUE
print(x == y)  # FALSE
print((x > 5) & (y < 10))  # TRUE & TRUE = TRUE
print(!(x > y))            # !TRUE = FALSE

quizData.question: What is the result of `(4 > 3) | (2 == 5)`?
quizData.explanation: 4 > 3 is TRUE, 2 == 5 is FALSE. TRUE | FALSE = TRUE.

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content:
Logical values are often coerced to numbers in calculations: TRUE becomes 1, FALSE becomes 0. This is useful for counting:

```r
sum(c(TRUE, FALSE, TRUE))  # returns 2
```

slides.title: Check Your Understanding
slides.content: Create a variable `is_adult` that is TRUE if age is 18 or more. Set age to 20 and verify.
